section.title: The Birth of Obed
section.sectionRationale: The final movement resolves and crowns the whole book. Ruth bears a son, the empty Naomi is filled, the women bless the LORD who gave a redeemer, and the closing genealogy quietly reveals the point of it all: this child leads to David. It is one unit that turns a family's rescue into the hinge of Israel's, and the world's, redemptive history.
section.thesis: The LORD who emptied Naomi now fills her arms with a redeeming grandson, and the genealogy pulls back the curtain: the whole story of famine, loyalty, and redemption was God quietly preparing the line of David, and through David the Messiah.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the birth of Obed and the women's blessing of God; Naomi filled with the child on her lap; and the genealogy from Perez to David that discloses God's purpose.

units.label: The birth and the women's blessing

verses.text: So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. And when he had relations with her, the LORD enabled her to conceive, and she gave birth to a son.
Then the women said to Naomi, “Blessed be the LORD, who has not left you this day without a {{goel|kinsman-redeemer}}. May his name become famous in Israel.
He will renew your life and sustain you in your old age. For your daughter-in-law, who loves you and is better to you than seven sons, has given him birth.”

units.label: Naomi filled
units.note: a son has been born to Naomi

verses.text: And Naomi took the child, placed him on her lap, and became a nurse to him.
The neighbor women said, “A son has been born to Naomi,” and they named him {{obed|Obed}}. He became the father of Jesse, the father of {{david|David}}.
Now these are the {{toledot|generations}} of Perez: Perez was the father of Hezron,
Hezron was the father of Ram, Ram was the father of Amminadab,
Amminadab was the father of Nahshon, Nahshon was the father of Salmon,
Salmon was the father of Boaz, Boaz was the father of Obed,
Obed was the father of Jesse, and Jesse was the father of David.

goel.gloss: Kinsman-redeemer or guardian-redeemer, the near relative who rescues his kin and secures the family's future. Strikingly, the women call the newborn child Naomi's goel: the baby Obed secures her line and old age, an heir who redeems her from emptiness. The word crowns the theme that has run through the book and points to Christ, the Redeemer born in this very line.

obed.gloss: Obed, meaning one who serves or worships. The son of Ruth and Boaz is placed on Naomi's lap and named by the neighbor women; he becomes the grandfather of David. The empty widow is filled with a servant-son who secures her future and carries the line of promise forward.

david.gloss: David, whose name means beloved, Israel's greatest king. The whole book has been quietly climbing to this name: the story of famine, loyalty, and redemption was God's way of preparing the royal line. Beyond David stands the Messiah, great David's greater Son, Jesus, Matthew 1:1 and 1:5-6.

toledot.gloss: Generations, or an account of descendants. The same genealogical formula that structures the book of Genesis closes Ruth, tying this small, tender story into the great line of promise. Matthew 1 takes up these very names, placing Ruth and Boaz in the ancestry of Jesus the Messiah.

basic.context: Boaz marries Ruth, and God gives them a son. The women of Bethlehem bless the LORD and tell Naomi that this child is her redeemer, who will renew her life; they even say Ruth, who loves her, is better than seven sons. Naomi, once empty, now holds a grandson on her lap. The book ends with a short family tree revealing that this baby, Obed, is the grandfather of King David.
basic.hermeneutics: This ending reframes the whole book. What seemed a private tale of grief and rescue turns out to be a chapter in God's public plan for Israel and the world. The genealogy is not a dull appendix but the punchline: God was building the royal line all along. Naomi's fullness answers her emptiness in chapter one, closing the story's great reversal.
basic.originalLanguages: The women call the baby a goel, a redeemer, for Naomi, showing the child secures her future. He is named Obed, one who serves. The closing toledot, the account of generations, links Ruth to the great line, and the final word of the book is David, the name the whole story was quietly building toward.
basic.typology: Obed the redeemer-child who fills the empty and renews Naomi's life points to Christ, the child born to redeem and to give life. The line to David is the line to great David's greater Son. Ruth, an outsider in the Messiah's family tree, foreshadows the nations gathered into Christ.
basic.christ: The book ends by naming David, and the New Testament finishes the line: from David comes Jesus, Matthew 1. Ruth and Boaz are Christ's ancestors, and their story of redemption was God's quiet preparation for the great Redeemer. The child who renewed Naomi's life points to the Child who brings resurrection life to all His people.
basic.god: God, who seemed hidden through the whole book, is openly praised at the end: it is the LORD who gave conception and the redeemer. We learn that God fills the empty, that He weaves private sorrows into His public plan of salvation, and that His providence was steering every step toward the King and the Messiah.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 16 anoints David from this line. Matthew 1 carries it to Jesus. And Naomi's fullness echoes the God who lifts the poor and fills the hungry in 1 Samuel 2 and Luke 1.
basic.oneStory: The whole story lands here: the emptied widow is filled, and the child in her lap leads to David and to Jesus. Ruth's small story of loyalty and redemption turns out to be a vital link in the one great story of God redeeming a people and sending the King, the true Redeemer, born in Bethlehem.

context.literary: The resolution and climax that completes the book's great reversal from empty to full. The women's blessing answers Naomi's lament in chapter one word for word, replacing bitterness with fullness. The genealogy, ending emphatically on David, functions as the key that unlocks the meaning of everything that came before.
context.historical: Childbearing secured a widow's future and preserved a family's name and land, so Obed's birth is both deeply personal and legally significant. Naming David at the close would have thrilled the original readers, connecting their beloved king to a story of covenant loyalty and to a Moabite great-grandmother. The genealogy follows the standard ten-generation form from Perez, Judah's son, to David.
context.canonical: This ending binds Ruth to the whole canon: backward to Genesis through the toledot formula and the house of Perez, and forward to Samuel and the monarchy, and ultimately to Matthew's genealogy of Jesus. A book that began in the chaos of the judges ends by pointing to the king, and beyond him to the Messiah.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To resolve Naomi's emptiness with the fullness of a redeeming grandson, to give God open praise for the redemption He worked, and, through the genealogy, to reveal that the whole account was God's preparation of the royal line leading to David and the Messiah.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes God's provision of a child and a king; it does not prescribe a formula for reversing hardship. The abiding truths are that God fills the empty, that He works private faithfulness into His public purposes, and that His redemptive plan advances surely toward Christ.

debates.question: Why does the book end with a genealogy rather than a scene?

views.label: To reveal the book's true purpose
views.case: The genealogy is the point, not an afterthought: it discloses that this whole story of famine, loyalty, and redemption was God quietly building the line of David, giving the private drama national and redemptive significance.

views.label: To anchor Ruth in the covenant line
views.case: By tracing Perez to David, the closing verses tie Ruth and Boaz to Judah's promise and to Israel's greatest king, embedding the story in the ongoing plan of God rather than leaving it a self-contained tale.

debates.bearing: Either way the genealogy lifts the reader's eyes from one family's happy ending to God's grand design, which the New Testament completes in Jesus, the son of David, Matthew 1:1.

debates.question: In what sense is the child Obed called Naomi's redeemer?

views.label: The heir who secures her future
views.case: Obed carries on the family name and inheritance and will care for Naomi in old age, redeeming her from the emptiness and vulnerability of chapter one; the women rightly call him her goel.

views.label: A sign of God's redeeming faithfulness
views.case: The child embodies the LORD's covenant kindness to Naomi, the visible proof that God has not left her without a redeemer, so the term points beyond the boy to God's own redeeming work.

debates.bearing: Both senses hold together and point forward: God provides a redeemer to fill the empty, ultimately in Christ, the Redeemer born of this very line.

study.originalLanguages: The ending gathers up the book's key words. The women declare that the LORD has not left Naomi without a goel, a redeemer, and remarkably they apply the word to the newborn: the child himself secures her line and her old age. He is named Obed, one who serves or worships. The final unit is a toledot, an account of generations, the same formula that structures Genesis, tying this humble story into the great line of promise. And the very last word of the book is David, the name toward which the whole narrative has been quietly climbing, and beyond David, in the fullness of time, the Messiah.

typology.type: Obed the redeemer-child who fills the empty and renews Naomi's life
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Child born to redeem, who gives resurrection life and turns mourning into joy, Luke 2:11 and John 11:25.

typology.type: The line from Perez to David
typology.fulfillment: The royal line that leads to Jesus, the Son of David and King of kings, Matthew 1:1 and 1:5-6.

typology.type: Ruth the Moabitess in the Messiah's genealogy
typology.fulfillment: The nations gathered into Christ, grafted into the people and promises of God, Galatians 3:29.

study.christ: The book's final word, David, is the arrow that points to Christ. Matthew 1 completes the line: from Obed to Jesse to David, and on to Jesus, the Son of David, Matthew 1:5-6 and 1:1. Ruth and Boaz are therefore the ancestors of the Messiah, and their story of redemption was God's quiet preparation for the great Redeemer born in the same little town of Bethlehem. The child who renewed Naomi's life and filled her empty arms foreshadows the Child who brings everlasting life and fills the empty with good things. In Christ, the redeemer theme that has run through every chapter reaches its fulfillment.
study.god: Here, at last, God is openly praised for what He has been doing all along: it is the LORD who enabled Ruth to conceive and who gave Naomi a redeemer. We learn that God fills the empty and lifts up the lowly, that He weaves private faithfulness and ordinary lives into His grand public plan of salvation, and that His hidden providence throughout the book was steering every step toward the King and, beyond him, the Messiah. The God who seemed silent in Naomi's grief is the God whose faithfulness now overflows in blessing.

crossReferences.note: The LORD sends Samuel to Bethlehem to anoint a son of Jesse, of this very line, as king.
The genealogy of Jesus runs Boaz and Ruth to Obed, Jesse, and David, and on to the Christ.
Hannah's song: the barren has borne seven, and the LORD fills the hungry, echoing Naomi's reversal from empty to full.
Mary's song: He has filled the hungry with good things, the same God who fills Naomi and sends the Redeemer.

study.oneStory: The whole book lands here: the emptied widow is filled, and the child on her lap leads to David and at last to Jesus. Ruth's small story of loyalty and redemption proves to be a vital link in the one great story of God gathering a people and sending the King. A tale that opened in famine and death in the days of the judges closes by pointing to the true Redeemer, born in Bethlehem, who fills the empty and reigns forever.

sermon.title: The Mystery of History
sermon.why: The closing message of Begg's series A Study in Ruth, expounding Ruth 4:16-22, the naming of Obed and the genealogy to David, showing how this small story fits God's grand plan that leads to Christ. A faithful, orthodox handling of this movement's climax and the book's purpose.

quiz.q: Who is credited with enabling Ruth to conceive?

options.text: The LORD, who is openly praised as the giver of the child.
options.note: Ruth 4:13 says the LORD enabled her to conceive, naming God's hand at the climax.

options.text: Boaz, through his own strength.
options.note: Boaz is the husband, but the text credits the conception to the LORD.

options.text: Naomi, through her careful planning.
options.note: Naomi planned wisely, but Scripture gives the credit to God, not to her scheming.

options.text: The elders at the gate.
options.note: The elders blessed the marriage, but God is named as the one who gave the child.

quiz.q: What did the women of Bethlehem call the newborn child?

options.text: A kinsman-redeemer for Naomi, who would renew her life.
options.note: Ruth 4:14-15 calls the child Naomi's redeemer, filling her emptiness.

options.text: A burden that Naomi was too old to raise.
options.note: The child is celebrated as a blessing and redeemer, not a burden.

options.text: A replacement for Ruth in Naomi's affection.
options.note: The child renews Naomi, and Ruth is praised as better than seven sons, not replaced.

options.text: A child with no importance to the family line.
options.note: The child is central; he leads to David and the Messiah.

quiz.q: How is Ruth described in the women's blessing?

options.text: As one who loves Naomi and is better to her than seven sons.
options.note: Ruth 4:15 gives Ruth this striking praise, answering Naomi's earlier emptiness.

options.text: As a foreigner who could never truly belong.
options.note: The opposite: she is honored above seven sons and placed in the Messiah's line.

options.text: As a burden Naomi had to tolerate.
options.note: Ruth is celebrated as a loving daughter-in-law, not tolerated.

options.text: As someone who had failed Naomi.
options.note: Ruth is praised for her love and loyalty, not blamed for failure.

quiz.q: What does the closing genealogy reveal?

options.text: That the child Obed is the grandfather of King David, so the whole story was preparing the royal line.
options.note: Ruth 4:17-22 ends on David, disclosing the book's larger purpose.

options.text: That the family faded into obscurity.
options.note: The opposite: the line leads to David and, ultimately, to Christ.

options.text: That the story has no connection to the rest of the Bible.
options.note: The genealogy deliberately ties Ruth into the line of promise and the wider canon.

options.text: That Boaz was unrelated to David.
options.note: Boaz is David's great-grandfather in this very genealogy.

quiz.q: How does the end of Ruth point to Jesus?

options.text: The line leads to David, and from David comes Jesus, the Son of David, born in Bethlehem.
options.note: Matthew 1 completes the line from Boaz and Ruth to Christ.

options.text: It points only to David and stops there with no further meaning.
options.note: David is the immediate goal, but the New Testament carries the line to Jesus.

options.text: It shows that redemption was only ever about land.
options.note: The land redemption serves a larger purpose: preserving the line that leads to the Redeemer.

options.text: It has nothing to do with the coming Messiah.
options.note: The genealogy is exactly how Ruth connects to the Messiah in Matthew 1.

questions: Naomi went out full, came back empty, and now holds a grandson on her lap. Where do you long for God to fill an emptiness in your life, and how does Naomi's story build your hope?
The LORD, hidden through most of the book, is openly praised at the end. Looking back over a hard season, where can you now see God's faithful hand at work?
Ruth's quiet loyalty turned out to be a vital link in God's great plan. How does this encourage you that your ordinary faithfulness matters more than you can see?
A Moabite outsider became the great-grandmother of David and an ancestor of Jesus. How does God's welcome of outsiders shape your view of your own belonging in His family?
The whole story leads to Bethlehem and to Christ the Redeemer. How does seeing Jesus at the end of Ruth deepen your worship of Him as your Redeemer?

translationNotes.note: Kinsman-redeemer renders goel; strikingly it is applied to the newborn child, who secures Naomi's line and future, the BSB footnote pointing to the legal role of Leviticus 25.
Better to you than seven sons is the highest praise in a culture that prized sons; it answers Naomi's cry of emptiness in chapter one.
Obed means one who serves or worships; the neighbor women name him, and the verse leaps ahead to David, the goal of the whole book.
These are the generations renders the toledot formula that structures Genesis, tying Ruth into the line of promise; the same names appear in Matthew 1.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ruth 4:13-22. The genealogy in verses 18-22, set with line breaks in the source, is rendered here as running text within each verse per house style. Verse 20 follows the BSB reading Salmon, noted in the source with manuscript variants (Salma).
